title: Fondant sugar roses
subtitle: Rolling and shaping simple five-petal roses from ready-to-roll fondant
excerpt: A fondant rose is built petal by petal around a central cone. The petals are thinned at the edge by rolling between thumb and finger. Five petals is enough for a convincing open bloom; a few more gives a tighter bud. No special tools are needed beyond a small rolling pin and a paintbrush.
sourceNotes: Modelled sugar flowers from marzipan and sugar paste are documented in domestic confectionery manuals from the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, describes shaped sugar paste work and ornamental modelling. Mrs A.B. Marshall's confectionery works of the 1880s, 1890s give detailed instructions for moulded and hand-shaped sugar flowers. The method of wrapping petals around a central cone to form a rose is a standard technique in all subsequent cake-decorating literature and is fully public-domain.

content.text: Fondant roses take a little practice but improve quickly. Make a few in white before colouring, to learn the proportions without worrying about dye distribution. Colour the fondant before you start shaping, not after.

items.name: Fondant icing
items.prepNote: coloured with gel colouring if desired

content.text: Roll a small amount of {{fondant-icing}} into a ball about 1.5 cm across. Pinch one end to a blunt point to form a cone. This is the rose centre. Set it aside to firm for 10 minutes while you prepare the petals.
Roll a ball of fondant slightly smaller than the cone. Follow the

technique to form a disc thin at the edge and thicker at the base. Wrap the petal around the cone, pressing the base firmly to adhere. Add a second petal opposite the first. For each subsequent petal, slightly overlap the previous one and curl the thin edge back with your fingertip. Build up 5 to 7 petals for a full open rose. Pinch the base to a neat point and trim the excess.

Rest each finished rose on crumpled baking paper, which supports the petals in a natural curve as they dry. Leave for at least 2 hours at room temperature before placing on the cake. Push the trimmed base gently into buttercream or secure with a little royal icing.
